verses.text: Ruth goes out to glean, and 'her hap was to light on' the field of Boaz. To her it looks like chance; to God it is appointment. Behind what we carelessly call coincidence, the LORD is arranging every step of those who have come to trust in Him.

Ruth goes out to glean, and 'her hap was to light on' the field of Boaz. To her it looks like chance; to God it is appointment. Behind what we carelessly call coincidence, the LORD is arranging every step of those who have come to trust in Him.

Ruth goes out to glean, and 'her hap was to light on' the field of Boaz. To her it looks like chance; to God it is appointment. Behind what we carelessly call coincidence, the LORD is arranging every step of those who have come to trust in Him.

Boaz arrives greeting his workers, 'The LORD be with you,' and at once notices the stranger gleaning. A godly man carries God into his ordinary work and has eyes for the outsider. Grace begins moving toward Ruth before she has asked anyone for a thing.

Boaz arrives greeting his workers, 'The LORD be with you,' and at once notices the stranger gleaning. A godly man carries God into his ordinary work and has eyes for the outsider. Grace begins moving toward Ruth before she has asked anyone for a thing.

Boaz arrives greeting his workers, 'The LORD be with you,' and at once notices the stranger gleaning. A godly man carries God into his ordinary work and has eyes for the outsider. Grace begins moving toward Ruth before she has asked anyone for a thing.

Boaz tells Ruth to stay in his field, drink freely, glean unharmed. She falls on her face: 'Why have I found grace in thine eyes, seeing I am a stranger?' The wonder at unearned kindness is the first sign of a heart waking up to grace.

Boaz tells Ruth to stay in his field, drink freely, glean unharmed. She falls on her face: 'Why have I found grace in thine eyes, seeing I am a stranger?' The wonder at unearned kindness is the first sign of a heart waking up to grace.

Boaz tells Ruth to stay in his field, drink freely, glean unharmed. She falls on her face: 'Why have I found grace in thine eyes, seeing I am a stranger?' The wonder at unearned kindness is the first sign of a heart waking up to grace.

Boaz has heard how she left all to care for Naomi, and blesses her: 'a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust.' She came seeking grain and found refuge under the wings of God.

Boaz has heard how she left all to care for Naomi, and blesses her: 'a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust.' She came seeking grain and found refuge under the wings of God.

Boaz has heard how she left all to care for Naomi, and blesses her: 'a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust.' She came seeking grain and found refuge under the wings of God.

Boaz shares his own table with her, then quietly tells his men to pull out grain on purpose for her to find. True kindness goes past mere duty; it plans ahead to bless. He makes her gleaning easy without ever shaming her need.

Boaz shares his own table with her, then quietly tells his men to pull out grain on purpose for her to find. True kindness goes past mere duty; it plans ahead to bless. He makes her gleaning easy without ever shaming her need.

Ruth beats out an ephah of barley — far more than a day's gleaning — and brings it home. Naomi, astonished at the abundance, asks where she worked: 'blessed be he that did take knowledge of thee.' Generosity stirs gratitude and turns the eye back to God.

Ruth beats out an ephah of barley — far more than a day's gleaning — and brings it home. Naomi, astonished at the abundance, asks where she worked: 'blessed be he that did take knowledge of thee.' Generosity stirs gratitude and turns the eye back to God.

Ruth beats out an ephah of barley — far more than a day's gleaning — and brings it home. Naomi, astonished at the abundance, asks where she worked: 'blessed be he that did take knowledge of thee.' Generosity stirs gratitude and turns the eye back to God.

When Naomi hears the name Boaz, hope revives: 'the man is near of kin unto us, one of our next kinsmen.' That word means redeemer. So Ruth gleans safely through the whole harvest. God is quietly drawing a redeemer near to the empty house of Mara.
